accurate.why: Claims about reauthorizing first responder training grants under the PHSA and funding figures match the bill text and verified record.

no_hallucinations.why: No fabricated claims; the SAMHSA-administered FR-CARA program context and amounts are accurate.

states_purpose.why: Clearly identifies the bill's purpose as reauthorizing/expanding the first responder training grant program.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Correctly states the bill amends the Public Health Service Act and changes the prior authorization level.

exceptions_conditions.why: The bill contains no material exceptions or eligibility carve-outs requiring mention.

effective_dates.why: Correctly notes the FY2026–2030 authorization period as the relevant timing provision.

major_provisions_covered.why: The summary is truncated and fails to fully describe the key operative change broadening the program beyond opioids to general drug/overdose training and the 'otherwise legally marketed' device language.

objective_tone.why: The tone is neutral and non-partisan throughout.

coherent.why: The summary ends abruptly mid-sentence ('In addition to increasing funding, the'), leaving it incomplete and not internally finished.

concise.why: The text is free of padding or filler.

correct_entities.why: Correctly names the Public Health Service Act and the bill title and number.

correct_figures.why: Both the new $57,000,000 (FY2026–2030) and prior $36,000,000 (FY2019–2023) figures match the bill text.
